lesson.title: Mean Value Theorem
lesson.body: The Mean Value Theorem (MVT) states: if f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then there exists c ∈ (a,b) such that f'(c) = [f(b)−f(a)]/(b−a). Geometrically, the tangent line at c is parallel to the secant line. Rolle's Theorem is the special case f(a) = f(b) → f'(c) = 0.

rooms.question: The Mean Value Theorem guarantees some c in (a,b) with f'(c) equal to:
rooms.explanation: The MVT guarantees a point where the instantaneous rate equals the average rate over [a,b].

rooms.question: For f(x) = x² on [0, 2], the value c guaranteed by the MVT satisfies f'(c) = ?
Rolle's Theorem is a special case of the MVT. Its additional hypothesis (beyond MVT's) is:
rooms.explanation: Rolle's Theorem adds f(a) = f(b), which makes the average rate zero, so f'(c) = 0 for some c in (a,b).

rooms.question: Order the logical implications for a function on [a,b]:
items: f increasing on (a,b) iff f'(x) > 0 on (a,b)
f'(x) > 0 implies f is increasing (by MVT)
Critical points: f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) DNE
Test sign of f' on each interval between critical points
rooms.explanation: Find critical points first, then test sign of f' on each interval to determine increasing/decreasing behavior.

rooms.question: f is concave up on an interval if:
rooms.explanation: Concave up (bowl shape) ↔ f'' > 0. Concave down (arch shape) ↔ f'' < 0.

boss.name: The Mean Value Tyrant
boss.taunt: Somewhere in the interval, you touched the average. Prove it.

questions.question: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then by the MVT:
questions.explanation: MVT: for any x₁, x₂ ∈ (a,b), f(x₂)−f(x₁) = f'(c)(x₂−x₁) = 0. So f(x₁) = f(x₂) for all pairs → f is constant.

questions.question: A car's speed at t=0 is 0 and at t=3 is 90 mph. The MVT guarantees that at some t ∈ (0,3):
questions.explanation: MVT applied to velocity: average acceleration = (90−0)/3 = 30 mph/hr = f'(c) for some c ∈ (0,3).

questions.question: An inflection point at x = c requires:
answers: f''(c) = 0 AND f'' changes sign at c
questions.explanation: f''(c) = 0 is necessary but not sufficient. The second derivative must actually change sign at c for an inflection point.
